whatMustBeOnSite: Current hood cleaning certificate or kitchen label showing the last cleaning date and contractor.
Current hood suppression and other life-safety inspection reports that were submitted through Inspection Reports Online.
Operational permit paperwork or application records when the kitchen or event use requires a Fire Marshal permit.
Grease cleaning logs, manifests, and annual grease control certification records if the site operates grease control equipment.
commonFailureReasons: The site cannot produce hood cleaning, suppression, or other life-safety reports during inspection.
Hood or duct systems show excessive grease contamination or the required dated label is missing.
An operational permit or required uploaded documentation is missing before the inspection date.
Life-safety deficiencies remain open when the follow-up or recurring inspection occurs.
rescheduleMethod: Request inspections through Nashville's Fire and Life Safety Inspections page and use the Fire Marshal's Office contact workflow when a time-specific follow-up is needed.
penaltyOrEscalation: Nashville says operational permits can require one-time, regular, or recurring inspections, permit fees are non-refundable, and permits can be revoked if the permit terms or Fire Code requirements are not followed.
